title: Default Parameters 🛡️
description: Set default values so your methods don't crash when data is missing.

slides.title: Missing Arguments 😱
slides.content:
As we just learned, if a method demands an argument and you don't provide it, your program will instantly crash.

But what if you want to make an argument *optional*?

You can do this by assigning a **Default Parameter**.

slides.title: Setting a Default
slides.content:
Inside the parentheses of the method definition, you just use an `=` sign to set a default value.

```ruby
def greet(name = "Stranger")
  puts "Hello, #{name}!"
end
```

If the user provides an argument, it overwrites `"Stranger"`. If they don't, it safely falls back to `"Stranger"` without crashing!

slides.title: Defaults in Action
slides.content: Run this! See how the method handles both situations gracefully.
slides.codeSnippet:
def order_coffee(size = "Medium")
  puts "Here is your #{size} coffee!"
end

# Calling with no arguments
order_coffee()

# Calling with an argument
order_coffee("Large")

quizData.question: What happens if you provide an argument when a method already has a default parameter set?

options.text: It completely ignores your argument and uses the default.
The default value is ignored, and your argument takes its place.

quizData.explanation: Custom arguments always win. The default parameter only gets used if nothing else was provided.

slides.title: Mixing Parameters 🥣
slides.content:
You can mix normal parameters with default parameters! 

However, convention says you should put your mandatory parameters first, and all your optional (default) parameters at the very end.

```ruby
def register(email, username="Guest")
```

quizData.question: If you call `register("john@test.com")`, what is the username variable set to?
quizData.explanation: Because only one argument was provided, `email` grabbed it, and `username` safely fell back to its default.
